Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

description.narrative: 1.Area of landscape (excluding PAs) under improved practices to benefit biodiversity as measured by: - Completion of mapping and zoning, Establishment of landscape management strategy, and Functioning coordination platform for decision-making and measures in place for its integrated management 2. Indicator 2 (Ref: GEF Core Indicator 4.3): Area of degraded agricultural lands under sustainable land management in production systems 3.Number of direct project beneficiaries disaggregated by gender and measured by Average incomes of participating households. 4. conservation, sustainable use, and equitable access to and benefit sharing of natural resources, biodiversity and ecosystems as indicated by: (a) Policy frameworks (b) Legal and regulatory frameworks and (c) Institutional frameworks 5. Level of institutional capacities for planning, implementation and monitoring integrated landscape management planning as measured by UNDP’s capacity development scorecard comprising following agencies: NCSD, DBD, MOE, MAFF and GDANPC 6. Number of regional, provincial and local partners adopting the ILM framework to mainstream biodiversity into their planning systems as indicated by: (a) INRM guidelines adopted (b) Regional and local plans mainstreaming INRM and Biodiversity (c) Sectoral partnerships established for collaborative and integrated planning and management 7.Baseline METT scores: - KPWS : 33 - PKNP : 32 - Angkor PL: 59 8. Community-based NRM initiated and operational as indicated/measured by: Extent of Community Protected Areas (CPAs) and of Community Forests (CFs) established with (i) management plans including renewal of existing CPAs, and (ii) MOAs for co-management signed and under community management with budgetary allocations for implementation 9.Status of key species in the northern landscape as measured by increased number of nests protected and success rate over baseline values for: (i) Sarus Crane (ii) Giant Ibis (iii) Lesser adjutant

comment.narrative:
1. Individual parts of landscape managed through sectoral approaches 2.Agricultural lands under continued degradation due to poor management regimes and lack of proven and cost-effective methods of use and restoration 3. Baseline annual average incomes in project area assessed at US$ 850/year/ household from agricultural activities amongst participating households 4.Specific, targeted integrated biodiversity management planning and management regulations, guidelines and policies largely absent or rudimentary 5. Limited institutional for planning, implementation and monitoring of multiple use integrated planning and management in landscapes as measured by UNDP Capacity Development Scorecard baseline values of 19 6.Limited engagement of multiple partners mainstreaming biodiversity consideration into their planning systems 7.Terrestrial PAs under improved management effectiveness as measured by METT scorecard for following PAs: - 1.KPWS - 2. PKNP - 3. Angkor 8. Current CPA and CFs under co-management not fully effective due to lack of capacity, resources and extension support 9. Current baselines of success rates of protected nests (Sarus Crane 87% based on 96 nests protected; Giant Ibis 86.7% based on 60 nests protected and Lesser adjutant 94.4% based on 431 nests protected) . Key species nesting and success rates validated in Year 1 and monitored annually or bi-annually in defined locations 10. No information available of erosion and run-off rates for existing agricultural and land practices. Baselines for current erosion rates under selected existing land practices to be measured in Year 1 11. Commune Development Plans, Commune Investments Plans, District Development Plans and District Investment Plans have limited attention to mainstreaming biodiversity consideration into their planning systems
12. Coordinated outreach on conservation threats lacking. Limited awareness of impact unplanned development among general public. Baseline survey establ
1. Existing policy and regulation on integrated PA reviewed 2. PMU staff formed 3. Steering Committee established
1. Existing policy and regulation on integrated PA reviewed 2. PMU staff formed 3. Steering Committee established

1. Integrated landscape management frameworks agreed among all stakeholders including specific long-term conservation outcomes to be achieved 2. Mapping and zoning completed. 3. Landscape management strategy management developed 4. INRM Guidelines to facilitate increased engagement of partners in biodiversity mainstreaming into sub-national planning systems developed.

1. The existing policy, regulations, strategy, and institutional development in relation to integrated protected areas (PAs), biodiversity and ecosystem services, the water resource management and also series of management tools and guidelines for promoting landscape contribution of ecosystem services of three protected areas and three watersheds in the Northern Region of Cambodia developed, consulted and being finalized. 2. The mapping and zoning activities are being developed. 3. The landscape management strategy management is being initiated. The management plan for Kulen Promtep Wildlife Sanctuary (KPWS) drafted. 4.Six assessment reports completed with inputs from various stakeholders through validation and consultation workshops and meeting both in physical and virtual forms. Those assessment reports are on i). Biodiversity and Ecosystem Policies and Institutional Development, ii). Gender Policy Analysis in Natural Resources and Landscape Management, iii). Water Resource Management, iv). Potential of Agro-Ecological, v). Building Private Sector and Business Development for sustainable and forest landscape, and vi). Sustainable Land Management in the project target area. INRM grant guideline developed.

1. Integrated landscape management frameworks agreed among all stakeholders including specific long-term conservation outcomes to be achieved 2. Mapping and zoning completed. 3. Landscape management strategy management developed 4. INRM Guidelines to facilitate increased engagement of partners in biodiversity mainstreaming into sub-national planning systems developed. 5. All existing CPAs and CFs mapped, management effectiveness evaluated and proposals for improving conservation and sustainable NRM defined and agreed with communities

1. Gender Policy Analysis in Natural Resources and Landscape Management developed and aligned with the Gender Analysis and Action Plan of the prodoc. The womens roles, the responsibilities and practices in NRM, the access to and control over natural resource and assets, and the decision-making processes, are of importance for contributing to gender equality and creating equitable opportunities for women and men at all levels of engagement have been captured and analysed. 2. Mapping and zoning of the Protected Areas within the Project target especially Phnom Kulen National Park (PKNP) and Kulen Promtep Wildlife Sanctuary (KPWS) under the project intervention, implemented by pre-identified partners. 3. In terms of landscape management strategy for the integrated landscape management in the project target areas, a number of conceptualized options, mechanisms, and guidelines developed. Those include: i) Proposal for the enhancement of the three protected area ecosystem services at landscape level in the Northern Region of Cambodia. This proposal identifies effective strategy plan for better management of landscape, and provides options for improving PAs Services that contribute to landscape planning and management. ii) Tools for promoting landscape contribution of ecosystem services of three protected areas and three watersheds in the Northern Region of Cambodia. These tools play roles as coordination support systems for promoting the integrated landscape management at the project landscape level, iii) Guideline for mainstreaming biodiversity conservation and ecosystem services into land-use master plan at the sub-national level, which contributes to improving the habitat connectivity and ensuring the sustainable natural resources management of the protected areas and management of riparian areas. 4. Reference to the findings of the review of the existing land use planning within the project target areas, tools for promoting landscape contribution of ecosystem services of three protected areas and three watersheds in the Northern Region of Cambodia developed. These tools play roles as coordination support systems for promoting integrated landscape management and habitat connectivity at the project landscape level. 5. Potential CPAs and CFs in the target project areas identified, selected and included into the workplan of the pre-identified partners and Identified partners in order to enhance the effectiveness of natural resource management and conservation of biodiversity and ecosystems, while local livelihoods are of importance for the improvement.

description.narrative: This Project Initiation Plan aims to expand UNDP support to improve the resilience of Cambodia’s health system as per the upcoming national heath strategy, in close liaison with the Ministry of Health, WHO and other key partners. It will support health-related procurement, and will assess capacity needs for resilient health systems.

Indicators per approved project document on 20 March 2020: 1.1 Level of capacity of the Government (separately national and sub-national) demonstrated on the following areas: 'inclusiveness' and 'responsiveness' (Perception: proportion of participants in the civic engagement mechanisms who believe decision making is inclusive and responsive) 1.3 Level of sustainability of CSOs 1.4 Level the authorities’ attitude (verbal and behavioral) assessed by CSOs members interviewed (Likert scale: 5 points) 2.1 Number of engagement infrastructure supported (disaggregated by): National and Sub-national 2.2 Level of effectiveness of the existing infrastructure by users (disaggregated by): Sex, Age, Geographical locations (% of perception by users of the infrastructure if it is improved) 2.3 % of participation of the following populations in the infrastructure: Women’s groups, Youth’s groups, and Other marginalized groups 3.1 % of civil servants think of CSOs as equal partners 3.2 # of ideas that are co-designed and jointly implemented by the government and civil society

1. Release of 7.46 km2 of contaminated land through clearance activities 2. Develop work plan 2021 (VA) 3. Develop work plan 2021 (MRE) 4. 1 Commune 5. With the Work Plan 2020, GMAP will review and revise during the third quarter of 2020. 6. Management response to CDNA recommendations developed and ToR for Provincial Mine Action Coordinator position developed. 7. N/A

1.4.95km2 (As of September 2020) 2. Detailed work plan for 2021 has been finalised (VA). 3. Detailed work plan for 2021 has been finalised (MRE). 4. Data collection processes of the 30 minefields completed and PMS report finalised. 5. Gender Training Needs Assessment (TNA) for the CMAA, LogFrame, ToR for Gender team, revision of GMAP 2021-2025 completed. 6. ToR for the Provincial Mine Action Coordinator position completed. 7. N/A

1. 9.92km2 of land mine cleared and released to communities in BTB, BTC and PLN 2. 531 Minefields visited 3. 800 QA/QC visited 4. 2 trainings on gender in MA provided by gender team to CMAA’s, MAPUs, QMT, and operators and local authorities 5. 4 meetings with GT member conducted 6. 80 people (mine victim survivors or their immediate family members) completed the skills training 7. At least 500 people benefiting from mine clearance support 8. 80 health center staffs and 800 village health support group members are trained with first aid response 9. 8 of students are registered to study prosthetic and orthotic course at the Cambodian School of Prosthetics and Orthotics 10. 34 students are registered to study physiotherapy course at the University of Health Sciences 11. 10 Mirror Therapy trainings conducted, and 300 mirrors distributed to amputees in mine affected provinces of Battambang, Pailin and Banteay Meanchey 12. 5,000 local community members are aware of their rights and mine victims support services available to them through CMAA’s survivor network (disaggregated by gender)

1. As of November, 24.20 km2 (244%) of mine contaminated area cleared and released in BTB, BMC and PLN (13.6 km2 through clearance and 10.6 km2 through LR-NTS). 2. As of November, 661 minefields (124%) were visited to conduct non-technical survey 3. As of November, 1,371 QA/QC visits (171%) were conducted 4. At the 29 September 2021 GMAP 2021-2025 review meeting, GICHD agreed to provide this training, currently scheduled for Q1 2022. 5. Four meetings were conducted in February, June, September, and October. 6. Signed a Responsible Party Agreement with CDPO and skill training activity has been implemented from November 2021, targeting 175 beneficiaries in 2022. 7. As of November, 31,537 people (6307%) benefited from mine clearance intervention, including 16,106 women and girls, 549 persons with disabilities, and 266 students. 8. First-aid response training started from October, with the finalized training materials. From August to 7 December, TCF provided first aid training to 34 health center staffs (42.5%) and 257 village health support groups (32.13%). 9. Student registration runs from January to February each year. With the approval of KOICA at the end of Q1, 2021, this activity has been postponed to 2022. In Q4, KOICA has also approved the target to be set at 4 PO students. 10. Student registration runs from January to February each year. With the approval of KOICA at the end of Q1, 2021, this activity has been postponed to 2022. In Q4, KOICA has also approved the target to be set at 30 PT students. 11. Together with PWDF and TCF, planned activities based on work plan 2021 commenced from July 2021. From August to 7 December 2021, TCF provided 19 mirror therapy training sessions and distributed 444 mirrors to beneficiaries and health center (BPRC). 12. 25 volunteers commenced collecting QLS data in 3 project target provinces (BTB, BMC, and PLN) in July and will be ongoing until December 2021.

title.narrative: Area of mine-affected land released (169km2) of suspected hazardous areas in targeted villages are released through survey and clearance activities.

description.narrative: The Project will contract land reclamation non-technical survey and baseline survey (LRNTS+BLS ) teams to release land that does not require clearance and capture previously unidentified contaminated land in the target villages, thereby maximizing the utilization of Project resources. This is also in-line with efficiencies outlined in the NMAS 2018-2025. The clearance methodology is: 1.1: Technical mine clearance and non-technical survey /land released 1.2: Number of people benefiting from land clearance.

description.narrative: 1.1. Number of public policy and surveys related to modelling of the socio-economic impacts of Covid informs government's stimulus package that protects both the economy and the most affected and vulnerable groups 1.2. Number of quick survey tools to assess economic conditions and impacts 2.1. Number of Poor Households affected by Covid 19 access to On Demand Identification poor household 2.2. Number of informal sector workers access to social insurance(the missing middle) 3.1 SPS protocols available to maintain flow of all physical goods with Viet Nam, Lao and Thailand 3.2 Acceleration of E-commerce uptake during and post Covid19 crisis and formalization of MSMEs in Cambodia 3.3 Australia-Cambodia Partnership for Consumer Protection

comment.narrative: - No survey conducted on MSME/ socio economic impacts - On-demand registration of Covid affected poor household not operationalized - No strategy for inclusion of missing middle/ informal sector workers (estimated at 4 million workers); no means of making COVID-19 support; No formal registry of informal sector workers (estimated at 4M workers) - No sanitary protocols for pandemic case - Onboarding of small producers and service providers in essential sectors not effective and not prioritized - Ad hoc, unstructured, administrative communication only

- Stimulus/ Covid response of least 3% of GDP, with clear focus on the most affected groups and vulnerable populations (% of stimulus). - Survey conducted for at least 2 sectors (MSME and informal sector) - On-demand registration of Covid affected poor household not operationalized in 25 municipality/ provinces - Basis of a strategy to tackle the SP challenges faced by the missing middle is in place; and Options are set-out to enroll informal sector workers in the social insurance system, enabling rapid disbursement of COVID-19 reliefs. - SPS Protocols developed and tested at Viet Nam, Laos and Thailand borders for flow of goods (3 border points) - 1). Small producers, sellers and service providers in essential sectors maintain continuity of business through online channels: 1000 businesses enrolled in marketplaces in 3 provinces (40 are women-led businesses); 2). 2,000 farmers connected to online agriculture platform providing access to easy to access agriculture supplies, technical and credit supports; 3). New jobs created: 500 (300 in PP, 100 in SRP, 100 in BTB, 50% are women)

- Completed COVID-19 Economic and Social Impact Assessment using CGE modelling and GTAP Stimulation, with a close partnership with MEF, to evaluate the impacts on economic growth, poverty and employment. - Launched and published Policy Brief in October 2020, with the support from DPM, Minister of Economy and Finance. - Economic modelling technical assistance has shaped the Government’s stimulus packages to support the most vulnerable people through social protection programmes. As a result, the RGC has moved quickly to put in place a set of policy measures and a stimulus package of around 4.3 percent of GDP (i.e., approximately US$1,164 million), including cash transfers to poor households (US$300 million), cash for work (US$100 million), wage subsidies to workers in garment and tourism sectors (US$64 million), and support to SMEs (US$600 million). - Completed the virtually delivery of the first-round training on macroeconomic modelling by one international and one national macroeconomic modellers to MEF’s team working on the national Covid-19 economic response. Certificate handout was held in October 2020. - The longitudinal, national surveys of informal workers and MSMEs consist of three waves, and Kantar Ltd was contracted to conduct this research project. The first wave was conducted in July 220 (n=1,392 for informal workers; n=514 for MSME surveys). The second one was completed in October 2020 while the third one will be done in January 2021. The survey data has been integrated into the interactive dashboard that will be soon made publicly accessible. - 1,700 tablets and software to the Ministry of Planning along with training for rapid and accurate registration of citizens into Cambodia’s social protection identification system. - 674,146 poor households affected by COVID-19 gained access to the Government’s cash transfer programme - Three regional trainings on OD-ID Poor and cash transfer programme, provided to the provincial departments of planning, PoSVY and commune/Sangkat. - Angkor Research Consulting Ltd has been recruited to conduct the socio-economic impact assessment of Covid-19 cash transfer program. To capture the effects of cash transfer over an extended period, a longitudinal tracer study (n=1,000) has been employed in this study on randomly selected Poor 1 / Poor 2 card holders and non-IDpoor holders at regular intervals. The fieldwork will be conducted in the fourth week of December. Follow-up phone interview will be administered in two waves—January 2020 and February 2021. - The rapid assessment has been conducted at 3 mainland borders checkpoints and 1 international airport custom and excise branch on the challenges and constraints hindering the flow of cross-border trading activities, as well as the risks imposing to associated persons working at the borders. - The internal guideline and sanitary and health protocol has already been drafted, while the personal protective equipment (PPE) and sanitary equipment has been procured in parallel and supplied to GDCE. Thereafter, the training to the GDCE officials and relevant associated persons at the land border and international airport will be conducted at the onset or virtually as deems necessary to ensure the application of safety measures which would contribute to the acceleration of the trade facilitation process. - Two assessments on the extra capacity needs of the E-commerce marketplace and the vendor readiness for e-commerce uptake are completed. The assessments aim to understand both the existing e-marketplace capacity to onboard more vendors and the readiness of vendors/SMEs for e-commerce uptake. 25 online marketplaces were interviewed, and one consultative workshop took place with approx. 30 representatives from business associations, local vendors/sellers, and e-marketplace to validate the study results. - Through boosting farmers’ income through access to agri-tech platform initiative, 1,200 smallholder farmers growing rice and vegetables, in five provinces, were identified and are registered in the digital application- called Agribee. These farmers are trained to use the application for various farming purposes such as purchase inputs, access to extension services, and sell their products. - In term of job creation, UNDP is working closely with the National Employment Agencies to organize a series of Career Forums both online and offline, taking into account the COVID-19 prevention measures, in early next year in three geographical areas including Phnom Penh, Siem Reap and Battambang to link idle capacity to the career opportunities which are newly created from this e-commerce uptake of MSME and the upscaling of the business operation of e-marketplaces.

- Stimulus/ Covid response of least 3% of GDP, with clear focus on the most affected groups and vulnerable populations (% of stimulus). - On-demand registration of Covid affected poor household not operationalized in 25 municipality/ provinces - Basis of a strategy to tackle the SP challenges faced by the missing middle is in place; and Options are set-out to enroll informal sector workers in the social insurance system, enabling rapid disbursement of COVID-19 reliefs. - Survey conducted for at least 2 sectors (MSME and informal sector) - 1). Small producers, sellers and service providers in essential sectors maintain continuity of business through online channels: 1000 businesses enrolled in marketplaces in 3 provinces (40 are women-led businesses); 2). 2,000 farmers connected to online agriculture platform providing access to easy to access agriculture supplies, technical and credit supports; 3). New jobs created: 500 (300 in PP, 100 in SRP, 100 in BTB, 50% are women) - Quarterly Microsoft Teams conferences, with a targeted agenda with standing and rolling agenda items.

A comprehensive modelling of the socio-economic impacts of COVID-19 by UNDP, in close partnership with the Ministry of Economy and Finance (MEF), led to the development of a second policy brief. The policy brief titled 2021 COVID-19 Economic and Social Impact Assessment in Cambodia, was launched and published jointly with MEF and DFAT. One of the key points of advocacy was social protection programming for the most vulnerable. The estimated 2021 stimulus package totalled US$1,454 million, equal to 5.1% of GDP , made towards the health, economic and social sectors, including cash transfers to poor and vulnerable households, and SME financing. The stimulus package for the social sector, including cash transfers to the poor and vulnerable households, increased to US$564 million in 2021 from US$464 million in 2020. From this, the cash transfer programme increased to US$500 million from US$300 million.
Building on the success of the emergency cash transfer programme in 2020, UNDP in collaboration with The World Bank and GIZ provided technical assistance to the Ministry of Planning (MoP) to recalibrate the methodology for identifying eligible households to be included in the ID Poor database. This contributed to the ID Poor system being more inclusive, with accuracy increasing by up to 74% (note: for internal use of this report only). As a result, an additional 6,374 Persons with Disabilities (49% are women with disabilities), and 7,783 People Living with HIV (PLHIV) (53% are women), were included in the national Social Protection Programme. The adjusted methodology enabled the identification of 108,000 vulnerable households to receive one off cash transfer support of $40-$75 per household during the 4-month lockdown in mid-2021.
As part of continuing efforts to address the “missing middle” problem, UNDP in partnership with ILO Cambodia, conducted a formalisation constraints survey alongside the fourth wave of informal workers, formal workers and MSME surveys in November 2021. Various formalization constraints and practical options have been identified as a possible pathway towards formalisation of the informal sector, potentially bringing them under the coverage of the National Social Security Fund.
As part of the initiatives related to the acceleration of E-commerce during the COVID-19 crisis and formalization of MSMEs in Cambodia, 1,427 MSMEs received capacity building trainings to transition to e-commerce platforms as a means to increase employment generation and economic resilience to shocks during the pandemic. This resulted in job continuity for 6,136 people (41% women) during COVID-19 outbreaks and lockdowns. The engagement of MSMEs in e-commerce will be further expanded through the Ministry of Post and Telecommunication’s (MPTC) agenda to enhance the digital economy.

1). On-demand registration of Covid affected poor households operationalized in 25 municipality/ provinces 2). Assessment on the impact of cash transfer to generate evidence to inform future cash transfer policy decisions 3). Research study and policy proposals for covering the missing middle – of informal sector workers, the self-employed and micro and small enterprises proprietors. 4). Development of practical options for employing the social security system 5). IDPoor complaint mechanism; M&E system and new IDpoor procedures/questionnaires 6). A multi-tiered pension system study

1). As of November 2022, more than 868 million has transferred to 702,907 households including 364,350 elderly, 64,242 persons with disabilities and 2,030 people living with HIV. 2). 20 in-deptUSD h interviews were carried out, covering IDpoor recipients and non-IDpoor recipients in urban and rural settings. The final report was published and launched jointly with the Ministry of Economy and Finance and the National Social Protection Council in July 2022. 3). The survey on formalization constraints and policy options for informal workers and MSMEs was completed as part of the 4th wave of informal workers and MSME surveys. The main findings shed light on characteristics of formal and informal enterprises/workers, incentives and disincentives of formalization, and identify some entry points to incentivize businesses to register and formalize. 4). The research project on integrated formalization strategy, aiming at identifying practical options and developing proposal to address the missing middle of informal workers and enterprises, has been conducted in partnership with ILO Cambodia and GS-NSPC in 2022. Through consultation with key government counterparts and high-level policy dialogue, the study envisions bringing these key stakeholders together to develop incentives for formalization and reduce barriers of formalization. The final report has been finalized. The report will be used to guide the development of the Common Country Strategy on Formalization. Joint workplan of UNDP, ILO and GS-NSPC on integrated formalization strategy was finalized with a clear direction to moving forward on the integrated formalization in Cambodia. 5). On Demand IDPoor has been rolled out successfully and the revision of ID poor questionnaires and procedures were finalized and launched in September 2022. The new procedures include (I) identify poor households and update DPoor data, (II) process and data quality monitoring as well as (III) objection and suggestion management procedures (complaint mechanism). The ID Poor system has been extended to the at-risk households (near-poor households) for the Covid-19 Cash transfer programme. The Covid-19 Cash Transfer programme will be extended until the end of 2023. 6). The multi-tiered pension system study was completed in 2022 in collaboration with ILO Cambodia to examine the possibility of introducing social protection floor to ensure that older persons that are ineligible for retirement pensions or receive them in an inadequate amount and are living without care from family members obtain appropriate incomes, aiming at preventing them from falling into poverty.

The project will have a 3-pronged approach aimed at enhancing the rights of persons with disabilities in Cambodia. On a systemic level, the project will support inclusive processes of consultation, drafting, enactment and implementation of the new rights-based Disability Law. The new law will be a structural change which will hopefully represent the cornerstone for building a more rights-based and disability inclusive society in Cambodia.

description.narrative:
Outcome 1: 1). # of trainings (disaggregation by type of capacity building) developed and delivered in the UNPRPD programme. (Disaggregated by topics) 2). # of participants (disaggregated by type of stakeholder) disaggregated by sex, disability, rural/urban participating in capacity building activities funded or provided by UNPRPD programmes 3). # of knowledge products (disaggregated by type of product /Thematic focus) developed, piloted and disseminated to the relevant stakeholders to inform inclusive practices 4). # of mechanisms to share and exchange learning and evidence to inform inclusive policies and systemsm(disaggregated by national/ regional/global mechanism).
Outcome 2: 1). # of national regulatory frameworks and systems changes targeted by the UNPRPD program 2) # of multi-stakeholder coordination mechanisms (disaggregated formal/informal) to support legislative policy and systems changes developed or strengthened 3). # Stakeholders involved in consultation and validation processes (disaggregation by stakeholder (GOV/ UN/OPDs/other)
Outcome 3: 1).# of adopted/ implemented COVID-19 inclusive response and recovery plans and frameworks containing systematic mainstreaming of persons with disabilities including the most marginalised 2) # of identified persons with disabilities including through their representative organizations participating in the State’s formulation/implementation of COVID-19 policy responses

comment.narrative: - Project proposal approved by the UNPRPD Secretariat - National Disability law - Practical guideline on Access to Legal Aid for Persons with Disabilities - Vocational training guideline for persons with disabilities - Draft Law on Access to Information - National and sub national disability machinery

- Finalise UNJP programme document - Organise LPAC meeting - Organise the 1st UNJP Steering Committee Meeting - Grant agreements with project partners - Finalise the draft disability law - Review NDSP2, 2019-2023 and develop the new NDSP3, 2024-2029

- The UNJP programme document finalised and approved by the UN participating agencies and the Royal Government of Cambodia. - The LPAC meeting was organised and endorsed on the UNJP named Accelerating Disability Rights in Cambodia (ADRC). - The first UNDP Steering Commitee Meeting was conducted and approved on the annual workplan 2022 as well as to endorse on the members of Steering Committee, the two Co-Chairs - Secretary of State of the Ministry of Social Affairs Veterans and Youth Rehabilitation (MoSVY) and UN Resident Coordinator, head of the three agencies are the members together with the representative of Ministry of Economic and Finance (MEF), representatives from persons with disabilities. - Two grant agreements were signed and kicked off its implementation. - The draft disability law was finalised and under the review of General Department of the Social Policy before submission to the Council of Minister for review and finalising it. - The NDSP 2 was reviewed and reflected the progress, challenges and lessons learnt identified. The recommendations were highlighted for the next NDSP 3.

title.narrative: Enhanced capacity of multi-stakeholders at the national and subnational level in charge of disability mechanisms (Disability Action Council, Provincial Disability Action Council and Disability Action Working Group at line ministries) to support implementation and monitoring of disability-inclusive policies (Social protection), laws (Disability Law, Law on Access to information) and plans (National Disability Strategic Plan 2-NDSP2).

description.narrative: - # of trainings (disaggregation by type of capacity building) developed and delivered in the UNPRPD programme. description.narrative: 1. Extent of progress in for the development of a robust Information Systems and Decision Support as part of the “Cambodian Environmental Management Information System” (CEMIS). 2. Level of technical input/advice and other required support for the design of sustainable financing mechanisms effectiveness 3. Level of technical input/advice and other required support for Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) effectiveness

comment.narrative: 1. 1= Limited extent: initial function of CEMIS 2. 1= Limited effective: initial assessment and analysis 3. 1= Limited effective: initial M&E functioning

1. 2= Moderate extent: DGIS team conducted an inventory of existing spatial data to be used in ISDS and developed an open-source platform. 2. 2= Moderate effective: examined existing revenue sources and gaps for protected area management and reviewed options for sustainable finance mechanism. 3. 2= Moderate effective: Developed annual, semi-annual and quarterly work plans, M&E and communication plans. Supported designing and completion of baseline survey questions and data collection, entry and analysis.

1. 2= Moderate extent: Four multi-stakeholder consultation meetings were conducted in collaboration with UNDP-led Essential Life Support Areas (ELSA) initiative to discuss how an environmental monitoring system can be developed through integration of various spatial database to identify and monitor nature-based solutions for biodiversity, climate and sustainable development in Cambodia.
2. 2= Moderate effective: An initial work plan and schedules for sustainable financing work has been discussed with and agreed by the Ministry of Environment. Work is being focused on 1) Examination of existing revenue sources and gaps for protected area management in the country; 2) Review of existing economic valuation studies on specific ecosystems in CSLEP and identification of key ecosystems to be targeted under sustainable financing options; 3) Identification and prioritization of potential sustainable finance mechanisms and/or sources to mobilize financial resources.
3. 2= Moderate effective: A “tracking tool” has been developed and shared with the national consultants responsible for coordinating the implementation of each component to coordinate with their respective teams to fill the tracking tool with the relevant information and report back to the CSLEP/PIU.
1. 2= Moderate extent 2. 2= Moderate effective 3. 2= Moderate effective

1. 2= Moderate extent: • A beta-version of CEMIS platform developed • Procurement completed and IT/Network System is installed • Agreement on three-tier models/products for the Information Spatial Decision Support System (ISDS) on Protected Area Zoning drafted
2. 2= Moderate effective: A set of preliminary action steps (roadmap) for priority sustainable finance mechanisms identified
3. 2= Moderate effective: A set of project M&E requirements including Monitoring Effectiveness Tracking Tool (METT), projects beneficiaries’ satisfaction, job creation indicators and reporting drafted.

description.narrative:

description.narrative: N/A - Project approved in December 2019

comment.narrative: N/A - Project approved in December 2019
RCT protocol, Project operation manual developed and project localities selected 1 Project Board Meeting 2 contract of specialized facilities (1. Graduation related works and 2. Cash transfer)

Two contracts with technical consultants - Economist and Graduation Based specialist who have a strong experience in RCT and graduation based social protection were completed.
RCT protocol and project operational manual was developed and being finalized by the technical consultant. The manual will provide a guidance for the project to implement the graduation based social protection that described comprehensively on how to implement it effectively.
The first project board the formal constitution of the project board composing of co-chairs and members representing from all relevant ministries and key stakeholders. Furthermore, the project board also agreed that the project board meeting can convene with flexibility as the situation required and as needed/ deemed necessary.
The project localities analysis was done by the Technical Consultant. The project localities will be implemented at Kampong Chnang and Preah Vihear provinces where there are less development activities and fit to the graduation based project.
The grant agreement with WVI was signed off while the letter of agreement with MoSVY is on-going.

Design of local economic survey Baseline survey is completed Selection and assignment of project participants based on random sampling and RCT protocol Start of poverty graduation activities, called 3 month plans for building livelihood foundation 1). Consumption support via monthly cash transfer 2). Life skills (financial inclusion and saving) 3). Selection of income generating activities (asset groups) 4). Livelihood training start from poverty graduation visioning and having a pathway

Concept of local area economic survey was done. The roll out of the survey needs to be decided due to budget constraint Two provincial working groups were constituted and functioned. The first meeting were organized in November 2021 2,966 IDPoor households were interviewed to assess their eligibility to be project participants 2,448 IDPoor households were selected to be project participants and assigned into asset, cash and control groups 1,587 IDPoor households already received consumption support via monthly cash transfer in October and November 2021 1,587 Wing Cards were distributed and saving accounts were opened for project participants (treatment) 90 percent of project participants (treatment groups) receiving project induction and training on family visioning, saving and financial management Asset identification and income generating plan of the asset group is completed

1). Analyze data from the baseline survey and select those meeting criteria to be project participants, and conduct random assignments (treatment and control groups) 2). Conduct early impact assessment 3). Provide literacy training/financial inclusion to both treatment groups 4). Provide behavior change communication to both treatment groups 5). Analyze and develop advocacy papers and proposals

1). Assignment of project participants into treatment and control groups completed: asset group (799 households), cash group (788 households) and control group (827 households). 799 households already received their productive assets (100 percent); 799 households in asset groups received technical assistance and training based on their income generating activities (productive assets); monthly cash for consumption support transferred to 1,587 households. 788 households in the cash group (100%) received one off lump sum cash to start/expand income generating activities. 2). Early impact assessment conducted, and report is being finalised. 3). 1,587 households received refresher training on basic financial management/saving. 4). 1,587 households received refresher training on family visioning (poverty graduation pathway), business plan and start up, life skills (hygiene, sanitation, household cleaning), and promoting child education. 5). Concept note for scaling up the project developed and consulted with the government for government cost sharing opportunity.

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

Number of beneficiary households of adaptation and mitigation activities supported by target ministries (and number of beneficiaries disaggregated by sex) 2.5.1. Number of meetings of the climate change technical working group on climate change per year, including number of meetings with development partners 2.5.2 Level of engagement of targeted sectors in CCTWG meetings, including meetings for update on national M&E indicators 2.6.1. Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC) impleme

comment.narrative:
1.2.3: 2. Two research partnerships have been finalized (heat stress, soil organic carbon management), both involving triangular cooperation between Government, local universities and international universities. One additional proposal is under development (adaptation costing in the infrastructure sector).
2.6.1: Yes. The validation workshop for the NDC was conducted in September 2020 engaging the CCTWG members including DPs, CSOs, private sector, and academia. Cambodia’s updated NDC has now been submitted for Government approval. It includes 33 priority actions for mitigation, 58 for adaptation, and 28 enabling activities.
3.3.1: 6. Six full proposals under the CCCA3 innovation grant facility (Round 1) received no-objection from the PEB members and are now under implementation. An additional COVID-19 grant was also approved by the board and is operational. Sectors covered: early warning systems, climate-resilient access to water and hygiene, energy efficiency in buildings, e-mobility, climate-smart community forestry, climate-friendly agriculture (including ethnic minorities), low-carbon irrigation technology.

1.2.3: Achieved (4). Four research partnerships are under implementation (heat stress, soil carbon management, adaptation costing in the infrastructure sector, and vulnerability assessment in the coastal areas)
2.6.1: Yes. The key data for the MRV/NDC tracking system had been collected from the line ministries, and the focal points were designated by target ministries. The training on the NDC Tracking System have been provided. The final private sector engagement plan has been finalized.
3.2.2: Achieved (3). Import tax on electric vehicles has been reduced by MEF in February 2021. For public investments, Ministry of Rural Development adopted new technical guidelines for resilient water infrastructure. The Long-Term Strategy for Carbon Neutrality in Cambodia (LTS4CN) was submitted to UNFCCC.
3.3.1: Achieved (12). 11 innovation grants (Round1 &2) are operational and 1 COVID grant is completed. 2 additional grants were awarded, for a combination of climate change and COVID19 activities: (1) RECOFTC (community forestry) and (2) TS-1001 (distribution of water and hygiene kits). Activities cover e-mobility, energy efficiency, early warning systems, climate smart agriculture and irrigation, switch to electric cooking, community forestry, resilient access to water, and industrial waste water treatment.

1.3.1: Achieved (8). The Institutional Readiness Indicator, Climate Finance Data Portal, vulnerability index, families affected, GHG emission projects registry have been updated respectively in 2019, 2020, and 2021 and the GHG inventory data and KAP 3 data has been added in 2021. The NDC/LTS4CN tracking system developed and upgraded in 2022
2.7.1: Yes. Ongoing. The focus is on M&E systems, and this is currently under development (expected completion in 2023)
3.2.1: 5 - 4 additional: Completed Assessing the Potential Impacts of Fiscal Measures to Reduce Import Duties/Taxes on Electric Motorbikes, Study on intermodal transport master plan completed, Economic analysis of Green Urban Infrastructure in Phnom Penh (presented to MEF, MLMUPC and PP municipality), A scoping study on carbon pricing options in Cambodia (in cooperation with regional programme of UNDP-Sweden), and In cooperation / co-financing with UNDP, supported a study on imports of second-hand cars (banning older vehicles).

title.narrative: Provision of appropriate equipment for on-site treatment of solid waste and wastewater using non-incineration and non-chemical technologies in accordance with WHO standards.

description.narrative: • Provision of appropriate equipment for on-site treatment of solid waste and wastewater using non-incineration and non-chemical technologies (e.g. microwaves, washing machines, sterilizer and shredder (e.g. sterilwave (440), wastewater treatment equipment) in accordance with WHO standards • Provision of equipment for the collection and segregation of waste and storage facilities (e.g. trolleys, color-coded plastic bags/disposal bins) • Provision of appropriate personal protective equipment (PPEs) to ensure safe waste handling

title.narrative: Support to the Royal Government of Cambodia in medical procurement and strengthening health systems

description.narrative: For the past few years, UNDP in collaboration with the World Health Organization (WHO), has been providing support to Cambodia’s health system through research and policy advocacy related to tobacco taxation, improving the quality-of-service delivery through user-informed design and digitalization, as well as lately through procurement of medical and laboratory equipment and other assets for the Government’s emergency health response to Covid-19 and sanitary disposal of Covid-19 waste. This Project Initiation Plan aims to expand UNDP support to improve the resilience of Cambodia’s health system as per the upcoming national health strategy, in close liaison with the Ministry of Health, WHO, and other key partners. It will support health-related procurement prioritized by the Ministry of Health (MoH), partnership management and the formulation of a full-fledged Health System Support Programme using a system approach in line with the upcoming CPD 2024-2028.

condition.narrative: The Implementing Partner shall ensure that all of its obligations set forth under the section entitled “Legal Context and/or Risk Management Standard Clauses” are passed on to each responsible party, subcontractor and sub-recipient and that all the clauses under this section entitled “Risk Management” are included, mutatis mutandis, in all sub-contracts or sub-agreements entered into further to the Project Document

title.narrative: Accelerating COVID-19 recovery for vulnerable and marginalized groups in Cambodia through providing assets for poverty reduction and resilience

description.narrative: The COVID-19 pandemic brought substantive impact to Cambodia’s economy and its people, particularly at-risk populations such as those living below or on the edge of the poverty line, workers in the informal sector (predominantly women), and individuals with disabilities. The project aims to improve the income-generating capacity of vulnerable rural households by supplying small-scale productive assets to improve the living conditions of impoverished households by providing goods that enhance the quality of life and healthcare, thereby improving hygiene practices.

title.narrative: Accelerating Nationally Determined Contributions (NDC 3.0) and promoting inclusive resilience in Nature, Climate and Energy (NCE) in Cambodia.

description.narrative: The proposed new Initiation Plan is (i) to support the preparation of the national climate pledges (Nationally Determined Contributions 3.0) and other climate strategies, while also creating synergies and platforms to effectively implement the current NCE pledges, and (ii) to provide a sufficient timeline for meaningful development and transition to the NCE portfolio, which is the instrument offers a new modality for UNDP Cambodia to engage with government and development partners on issues at a strategic level with focus on systematic perspectives to generate multiple options and maximize the impact of our work. For instance, the Circular Economy portfolio has been chosen as a pilot for portfolio design with strong support from CO’s management and guidance from the regional team, which will require time to complete the process by 2025.

title.narrative: Accelerating Energy Efficiency in Cambodia's Building Sector

description.narrative: This Joint Program (JP) aims to support Cambodia’s latest Updated Nationally Determined Contributions (NDC) to reduce 41% ofdomestic greenhouse gas emissions by 2030 compared to businessas usual, concentrated primarily in the Forest and Land Use, energy,agriculture, industry, and waste sectors. Accordingly, a general national goal to reduce total energy consumption was set at 19% by 2030, with a 25% reduction in the building sector in particular.

title.narrative: Decent Jobs and Social Protection Expansion for Sustainable and Inclusive Economic Growth in Cambodia (DJSP-JP)

description.narrative: Aligned with the Global Accelerator (GA) Roadmap and its theory of change, this Joint Programme (JP) has the overall goal of supporting the implementation of the corresponding Cambodia Roadmap for Jobs and Social Protection Acceleration. Accordingly, the JP will develop the national capacity for strategic foresight to identify future sources of growth that generate both income and jobs for fostering integrated national policies and strategies (GA Pillar 1) for building labour market flexibility, business resilience and competitiveness, and leveraging gender-responsive and inclusive partnerships, and integrated financing in the prioritized sectors with high potential for job creation (GA Pillar 2). The JP is expected to improve the wellbeing and livelihoods of at least 2.3 million beneficiaries and their children, and households living in extreme poverty. The JP follows an integrated and cross-sectoral approach to create an enabling environment and opportunities for decent jobs considering the interconnectedness among growth and job creation, skills, and social protection. The JP aligns with government priorities and focuses on providing technical assistance for the adoption of pro-employment macroeconomic and financing policies; improvement of skills for enhanced and inclusive employability; expansion of social protection coverage, particularly towards achieving UHC; support to business formalization, sectoral growth, and productivity; and enhancement of labour market inclusiveness. It is evidence-driven, and is designed to convene stakeholders, promote partnerships, and leverage integrated investments for enhanced multilateral cooperation (GA Pillar 3) and high return on investment. The value of this JP is above $4 million dollars with $30 million leverage.

title.narrative: PIMS-9519 Restoring ecosystems for sustainable development in the Tonle Sap Basin and Siem Reap/Phnom Kulen landscape(ERIP)

description.narrative: The objective of the project is to generate multiple climate, environmental, and socio-economic benefits at scale by enabling integrated collaborative management (restoration, rehabilitation, and conservation) of degraded watershed systems in Tonle Sap Basin. This will be achieved by disrupting the drivers of land degradation and promoting restoration through the integrated, collaborative management of watersheds in the TSB. This approach will involve three main components targeting policy and capacity; financing and livelihoods; and knowledge and communication, which together will provide global environmental benefits (GEBs) built on a foundation of scientific rigor, strong governance, and expanded investment.

condition.narrative: The Implementing Partner shall ensure that all of its obligations set forth under the section entitled “Legal Context and/or Risk Management Standard Clauses” are passed on to each responsible party, subcontractor and sub-recipient and that all the clauses under this section entitled “Risk Management” are included, mutatis mutandis, in all sub-contracts or sub-agreements entered into further to the Project Document

To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

title.narrative: Improvement of Disposal Capacity of Plastic Litter in Urban Provinces

description.narrative: The objective of the project is to support Cambodia’s efforts for preventing and minimizing plastic waste pollution on land and in the ocean through the promotion of a 4R (Refuse, Reduce, Reuse, and Recycle) framework. Building on the Phase I results, the Phase II project will further develop and implement enabling policy and regulatory measures, coupled with strategic communication and equipment provision to trigger behavior changes towards adopting Circular Economy (CE) measures. The project will also target the private sector to drive innovation and promote an enabling environment for mobilizing finances for upscaling proven CE business models. It will share best practices and lessons learned for replication and scaling up.

title.narrative: PIMS-10184 GEF SGP 8th Operational Phase (Part 1) CAMBODIA

description.narrative: SGP-OP8 builds on 30 years of successful experience in empowering local CSOs and CBOs in community driven initiatives that have enhanced wellbeing, increased awareness and resilience, and generated global environmental benefits. With economic development pressures intensifying in the developing world and the associated rising inequalities, local communities, particularly vulnerable and disadvantaged groups are becoming increasingly marginalized. The objective of Part 1 of SGP OP8 is to engage local CSOs/CBOs in landscape-seascape approaches across the 99 participating countries, providing them access to knowledge and information, capacitating them through learning-by-doing, skills development, and delivering technical and grant assistance for interventions that enhance socioeconomic conditions and generate global environmental benefits.

title.narrative: Project Management and implementation support to ensure the project is operationalised and effective.

description.narrative: Project Management and Implement Support

While significant progress has been made towards achieving gender equality in Cambodia, there exist economic, cultural, social and other factors that continue to challenge the inclusion and empowerment of people of all genders in Cambodia. This project seeks to transform institutions to address the barriers to achieving gender equality, supported by tools, processes, research, evidence and the capacity required to support achieve long-term and sustainable progress. To reduce the institutional, economic, cultural and mental barriers holding back the achievement of gender equality, a holistic approach is required, bringing the right partners, including government, civil society and the private sector together to make a maximum impact. The result is an institutional ecosystem that is more gender-transformative, empowered by capacity and knowledge, and better able to mainstream gender to achieve real result for women and men in Cambodia.

Clearing for Results Phase V (CfRV} takes forward the work done under CfR phase IV, led by the Cambodian Mine Action and Victim Assistance Authority (CMAA} and UNDP, and supported by the governments of Australia, Luxembourg, New Zealand, Republic of Korea and UNDP core donors with parallel funding from the Royal Government of Cambodia. CfRV will advance mine clearance, victim assistance, including livelihoods, sector-wide capacity development, and enabling actions towards a mine-free Cambodia by 2030, as outlined in the forthcoming National Mine Action Policy (2026- 2035). CfRV builds on the achievements and lessons learned from previous project phases implemented since 2006. The CfRV deploys an integrated approach to mine clearance for human development through four interconnected outputs. Output 1 supports the protection of communities in targeted areas through land release operations and tailored risk education. Output 2 strengthens access to emergency assistance, healthcare, rehabilitation, and inclusive livelihood opportunities for explosive ordnance victims, including the vulnerable. Output 3 enhances the capacity of the CMAA to manage residual contamination, uphold international commitments, and actively contribute to international cooperation in the mine action sector. And Output 4 focuses on promoting the productive use of cleared land for sustainable livelihoods, local economic and social development.

description.narrative: The project Community Action for Sustainable Forests (CASFor) aims to promote the sustainable management of local forest resources and contribute to national commitments and global action on climate change mitigation and biodiversity conservation. CASFor adopts a market-driven participatory approach across its activities, leveraging sustainable market opportunities while protecting forest landscapes rehabilitating and managing forest resources and building livelihoods’ resilience. This collaborative investment will assist project partners, Community Forestry (CFs), and Community Protected Areas (CPAs) in tailoring their activities to align with market demand, understanding market requirements, identifying potential partners, and addressing existing barriers. Beyond forest products buyers, the engagement will extend to potential investors in the carbon market that could support forest restoration activities as part of the sustainable financing options. CASFor will support CFs and CPAs and local communities to better understand the status and potential of forest resources and to make informed decisions regarding management options through the forest data collection and analysis. Given the increase in the number and area of CFs/CPAs across the country, it is important to design a forest and land use monitoring system that can measure small-scale community-based forest operations and integrate them into national forest and land use monitoring systems. Therefore, national institutional capacities will be strengthened to assess the potential contribution of Community-Based Forest Management (CBFM) to climate change mitigation, which is now possible thanks to high resolution satellite data. The project will also support the integration of tools and CPA/CF activities into Cambodia Environment Management Information System (CEMIS). The project will strengthen existing monitoring systems in Cambodia as CEMIS. Based on a gap analysis of existing systems that will be conduc

title.narrative: Engagement Facility for the Implementation of the Country Programme Document 2024 – 2028 between the Royal Government of Cambodia and UNDP

description.narrative: The objective of the Engagement Facility (EF) is to support upstream policy results, evaluation and assessments, crisis responses and the testing of innovations with scale-up potential for SDG acceleration in the context of Cambodia and in alignment with the Country Programme Document 2022 – 2026. The Engagement Facility delivery mechanism will enable CO to:• Provide policy and technical advisory services to the Government of Cambodia, stakeholders and partners that will enable UNDP to accelerate SDGs and enhance UNDP Country Programme outcomes and national development priorities, and other international obligations committed by the Cambodia;• Establish and/or enhance strategic partnership with the Government of Cambodia, potential donors and private sector partners to mobilise resources with a high return of investment for the implementation of Country Programme;• Develop new pipeline of initiatives / projects that address corporate, regional and country priority issues of scaling innovation, including integrating programming, digital access, gender equality, SDG finance, climate actions, governance, and other priority areas.

Nearly 80% of Cambodia’s poor population resides in rural areas, where they are predominantly engaged in subsistence farming. Almost all of the agricultural output they produce is used for household consumption and is highly vulnerable to weather extremes, economic and family shocks. The overall vision of the project is to build institutional capacity both at the central and sub-national level to implement the national Graduation Based Social Protection (GBSP) program and support a durable pathway out of poverty for participating poor households through basic cash transfers for subsistence and the acquisition of productive assets to develop their livelihoods, promote self-sufficiency, and enhance resilience. During the project period, 45,000 households (approx. 225,000 individuals) will be directly supported. To achieve this, the initiative will deliver comprehensive, integrated support, including:• Cash assistance to stabilise household income.• Cash assistance to purchase productive assets to enable income-generating activities.• Skills development and vocational training tailored to local market needs.• Individualised coaching and mentoring to guide families through the graduation process.

title.narrative: PIMS-9833 Global Electronics Management (GEM) Program Child Project in Cambodia

description.narrative: Cambodia is characterized by a high and rapidly growing volume of e-waste with limited existing e-waste management infrastructure and a high reliance on the informal recycling sector. The country’s consumption of electronic and electric equipment (EEE) has drastically increased, leading to significant challenges to e-waste management. At present, e-waste is managed mostly by a large and scattered informal sector in an environmentally unsound manner. This has increased emissions of persistent organic pollutants (POPs) including unintentionally produced POPs (UPOPs) and polybrominated diphenyl ethers (PDBEs), as well as mercury and other toxic substances. The project will support Cambodia in its effort to curb the rapid increase in e-waste, and address otherongoing challenges, such as a lack of information and data, limited enforcement of the legal framework for the management of POPs, mercury, and e-waste, and lack of infrastructure capacity and private sector investment, amplifying the volumes of toxic emissions and negative impacts on human health and the environment. This project aims to improve resource circularity and reduce the release of hazardous chemicals and wastes in the electronics value chain by addressing transformation levers (policy, infrastructure, innovation and learning, and multistakeholder dialogues).

title.narrative: Promoting Circular Solutions for the Food and Beverage Sector to Tackle Plastic Pollution in Cambodia(Plastic IP)

description.narrative: The use of single-use plastics (SUPs) has rapidly accelerated in Cambodia, particularly in the food and beverage (F&B) and tourism sectors, resulting in negative impacts on human health, the environment, and the economy. The Royal Government of Cambodia has developed various policies and strategies to support a circular economy (CE). However, their implementation is hindered by a lack of enforceable regulations, financial instruments, capacity, and public awareness. To address these challenges, the project aims to catalyze CE approaches by: i) Strengthening the regulatory environment through policies and standards, ii) Mobilizing finance and engaging the private sector for innovation, iii) Promoting behavioural change among consumers and businesses, and iv) Ensuring national coordination and knowledge sharing. Target provinces include Phnom Penh, Siem Reap, and Preah Sihanouk for interventions in the hospitality, tourism, and F&B sectors. The project will attain the following four (4) project components: ? Project Component 1: Enabling a regulatory and policy environment; ? Project Component 2: Engagement of the F&B sector for innovation and mobilization of finance for CE measures; ? Project Component 3: Promotion of behavior and social change towards CE solutions among citizens and the F&B private sector andnd tourism industries ; ? Project Component 4: National and programme-level coordination, knowledge management and communication.
